examples.title: Algorithm: DBSCAN
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DBSCAN
INPUT: epsilon (ε), minPts, dataset
OUTPUT: clusters, noise points

BEGIN
 FOR EACH point p IN dataset
 IF p IS NOT visited
 mark p AS visited
 neighbors = getNeighbors(p, epsilon)
 IF size(neighbors) >= minPts
 cluster = new Cluster
 expandCluster(p, neighbors, cluster, epsilon, minPts)
 ELSE
 mark p AS noise
 END FOR
END

FUNCTION expandCluster(p, neighbors, cluster, epsilon, minPts)
 ADD p TO cluster
 FOR EACH point q IN neighbors
 IF q IS NOT visited
 mark q AS visited
 newNeighbors = getNeighbors(q, epsilon)
 IF size(newNeighbors) >= minPts
 ADD newNeighbors TO neighbors
 END IF
 END IF
 IF q IS NOT IN cluster
 ADD q TO cluster
 END FOR
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: The DBSCAN algorithm iterates over each point in the dataset, checking if it has been visited. If not, it marks the point as visited and retrieves its neighbors within the epsilon radius. If the number of neighbors is greater than or equal to minPts, it creates a new cluster and expands it by adding the point and its neighbors. If the number of neighbors is less than minPts, it marks the point as noise.

examples.title: Python: DBSCAN
examples.description: Python implementation of the DBSCAN algorithm
examples.explanation: This Python implementation of DBSCAN uses the scikit-learn library for nearest neighbors search. It iterates over each point in the dataset, checking if it has been visited. If not, it marks the point as visited and retrieves its neighbors within the epsilon radius. If the number of neighbors is greater than or equal to minPts, it creates a new cluster and expands it by adding the point and its neighbors. If the number of neighbors is less than minPts, it marks the point as noise.
